request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"knewton","one_liner":"Knewton was a US-based B2B adaptive learning platform (founded 2008) that licensed AI-driven personalization technology to publishers and institutions; acquired by Wiley in 2019 for under $17M after raising ~$157M.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Knewton was a B2B SaaS platform sold to enterprises (publishers, universities) on recurring subscription contracts. The new pitch is a B2C tiered freemium app targeting individual K-12 consumers. Both involve recurring revenue and adaptive learning tech, but the customer and pricing structure are fundamentally different."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both operate in the edtech/adaptive-learning space and are ultimately trying to improve student outcomes with personalized instruction. Knewton focused on higher-ed and STEM in the US; the new pitch targets K-12 in Indonesia and the Philippines. Overlapping macro-market (edtech, adaptive learning) but different sub-segments and geographies."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton went to market via institutional partnerships (Pearson, ASU, Houghton Mifflin). The new pitch targets consumers directly via a mobile app with a freemium funnel. GTM motions are almost opposite — enterprise sales cycle vs. viral/organic consumer acquisition."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton was a well-funded, late-stage US startup that had raised $157M before failure. The new pitch appears early-stage. Both are pre-profitability growth-stage edtech plays, but Knewton's scale and burn were far larger."}},"why_similar":"Both Knewton and the new pitch are built on adaptive/AI-driven personalization of learning content. Knewton's core value proposition — identifying each student's strengths and weaknesses and making custom recommendations — is conceptually identical to what the new pitch calls 'AI tutoring.' Both are also targeting a world where one-size-fits-all instruction is replaced by technology-driven customization, and both involve recurring-revenue models layered on top of that personalization engine.","where_diverged":"Multiple concrete dimensions diverge: (1) Customer type — Knewton sold to enterprises (publishers, universities); the new pitch sells directly to consumers (K-12 students/families). (2) Geography — Knewton was US-focused; the new pitch targets Indonesia and the Philippines, emerging markets with very different willingness-to-pay and infrastructure. (3) Monetization — Knewton used pure B2B subscription; the new pitch uses tiered freemium with a consumer base. (4) Go-to-market — Knewton relied on institutional partnership deals (e.g., Pearson); the new pitch appears to rely on direct app-store distribution and community-based small-group sessions. (5) Educational level — Knewton concentrated on higher-ed STEM; the new pitch targets K-12.","failure_causes":["over-reliance on a small number of large publisher/institutional partners","B2B sales cycles too long relative to burn rate","inability to convert platform technology into direct end-user revenue","acquired for under $17M against ~$157M raised — severe value destruction suggesting poor unit economics or inability to scale","failure to reach individual learners directly, leaving revenue dependent on intermediaries","late pivot to direct courseware too slow to offset platform commoditization"],"lessons_for_input":["Validate willingness-to-pay early — emerging-market consumers have low ARPU ceilings, so model unit economics before scaling acquisition spend.","Own the end-user relationship from day one; do not rely on institutional intermediaries who can defund or de-prioritize your technology.","Keep the free tier narrow enough to drive genuine conversion pressure, not just usage; Knewton's B2B model never had to solve this but consumer freemium makes it existential.","Design the AI tutoring loop so that retention metrics (weekly active use, session completion) are measurable from MVP — adaptive learning only compounds if students keep returning.","Validate that local curriculum alignment (Indonesian and Philippine national syllabi) is tight before spending on AI personalization; misaligned content killed engagement in comparable plays."]}
